scenario: A manual quality-review process consists of three steps that a human reviewer performs in any order they prefer: visual inspection, document check, sample test. All three must complete before the process continues, but the order is not constrained.
question: Which BPMN construct fits?

options.text: Ad-hoc Subprocess with three User Tasks inside is the BPMN construct designed for unordered human-driven activities. Within an Ad-hoc Subprocess, all inner activities are available and the performer chooses when and which to execute. Completion is governed by a configurable completionCondition â€” for example, when all three tasks are done. This is the canonical BPMN construct for flexible human workflows where the performer has discretion over task ordering, precisely matching the quality-review scenario.

Parallel Gateway split with three User Tasks and a Parallel Gateway join allows all three tasks to complete in any order â€” the join waits for all three tokens. A human reviewer does them one at a time in any sequence. Ad-hoc Subprocess better represents the semantic intent: one reviewer performing tasks at their discretion, not three concurrent parallel work items that could be claimed by different workers simultaneously.

Sequential Multi-Instance iterates over a collection of similar items one at a time in a fixed sequence. The three quality review tasks â€” visual inspection, document check, sample test â€” are distinct activities with different inputs, outputs, and completion criteria; they are not homogeneous collection items. Multi-Instance imposes sequential order and a uniform task template, misrepresenting the flexible, unordered, heterogeneous nature of this human-driven quality review workflow where order discretion is the key requirement.

Three User Tasks in sequence with conditional skipping imposes a fixed order on tasks the reviewer is supposed to perform in any sequence. Even with conditional skip paths, the sequence structure communicates a preferred order to tools, monitoring systems, and other modelers. The requirement explicitly states order is not constrained. Ad-hoc Subprocess communicates unordered semantics unambiguously without requiring conditional bypass flows on every task, cleanly representing the reviewer discretion over execution order. ###

a.text: Correct. Ad-hoc Subprocess is the BPMN construct for unordered tasks -- performer chooses when/which to do. All inner activities are available; completion is governed by completionCondition. Designed for human-driven flexible flows.

b.text: Incorrect. Parallel Gateway implies all three start simultaneously and run in parallel; in practice a human does them serially but the model misrepresents that they're flexibly-ordered.

c.text: Incorrect. Multi-Instance iterates over a collection of similar work; here the three tasks are distinct.

d.text: Incorrect. Sequence implies order; the requirement is "in any order."

explanation: Ad-hoc Subprocess is the BPMN construct for unordered tasks -- performer chooses when/which to do. All inner activities are available; completion is governed by completionCondition. Designed for human-driven flexible flows.
